does: This paper introduces Abstract Gradient Training (AGT), a unified certification framework that formally bounds how much a machine learning model's parameters can change when training data is perturbed, removed, or augmented. AGT works by establishing provable parameter-space bounds and reachable sets of parameters under different training data perturbation scenarios. The framework addresses three distinct threats simultaneously: adversarial data poisoning where attackers corrupt training samples, machine unlearning where data must be forgotten, and differential privacy where individual data points are substituted. By analyzing gradient trajectories through abstract interpretation, AGT provides certificates that models remain robust to these training-time attacks, complementing the well-developed field of inference-time adversarial robustness certification.
connects: This work extends the landscape of machine learning robustness certification beyond inference-time defenses to the largely unexplored problem of training-time security. While adversarial robustness has generated extensive certification techniques like randomized smoothing and convex relaxation methods, certifying against training data perturbations has received limited attention. AGT bridges three previously separate research threads: data poisoning defense, differential privacy guarantees, and machine unlearning verification. The framework builds on gradient-based analysis methods and abstract interpretation techniques from program verification, applying these formal methods to establish rigorous guarantees in the parameter space rather than just output predictions, unifying what have been treated as distinct problems requiring separate solutions.
enables: AGT opens avenues for developing more trustworthy machine learning systems that can formally guarantee behavior under training data manipulation. This enables practitioners to certify models deployed in high-stakes domains where data integrity cannot be assumed and selective forgetting must be provably guaranteed. The unified framework could facilitate developing practical defenses combining poisoning robustness with differential privacy simultaneously, addressing multi-faceted threats in federated learning and privacy-preserving machine learning. Future work could extend AGT to more complex architectures, tighten parameter bounds to reduce certification overhead, and investigate whether parameter-space guarantees can be strengthened to provide output-space certificates, potentially advancing the intersection of formal verification and modern deep learning certification.
